data.title: Chris Larsen Cashes Out: $764M In XRP Profits Since 2018
data.description:
XRP continues to struggle for bullish momentum as market sentiment remains heavily bearish. After weeks of declining prices and fading trading volume, the token is still trading below the $2.5 mark, with traders growing increasingly cautious. While some analysts interpret this as a cooling-off period before the next potential expansion phase, fear and uncertainty continue to dominate the market’s short-term outlook.

Adding to the tension, top CryptoQuant analyst Maartunn revealed that Chris Larsen, Ripple’s co-founder, has realized over $764 million in profits since January 2018 from XRP-related sales. According to on-chain data, Larsen’s selling activity tends to coincide with local price peaks — a pattern that raises questions about whether current market behavior could signal another turning point.

Although such sales are not uncommon among large holders, timing and consistency are key factors that often influence investor sentiment. For many, these moves highlight the delicate balance between long-term strategic profit-taking and the perception of insider confidence in the project’s future. As XRP battles to hold current levels, the market will be closely watching whether institutional players and insiders maintain their exposure — or continue to cash out amid growing volatility.

Chris Larsen’s Recurring Profit-Taking and the Fragile State of Altcoins

According to analyst Maartunn, Chris Larsen’s latest XRP sale is connected to EvernorthXRP, an entity believed to be one of the wallets managing Ripple-linked holdings and distributions. While this particular transaction might appear routine, Maartunn points out that it fits a recurring pattern — Larsen has consistently realized large profits close to local market highs. Each time XRP experiences a rally, significant selling activity from wallets tied to Ripple executives tends to follow.

This recurring behavior fuels debate around insider timing and investor sentiment. While such moves can be interpreted as simple portfolio rebalancing, they often occur when retail enthusiasm peaks, amplifying uncertainty during already fragile market conditions. The timing of Larsen’s sales — amid a broader altcoin correction — has intensified speculation that large holders are preparing for extended market weakness.

The current environment for altcoins remains particularly delicate. Many tokens are sitting near long-term support zones, trading well below their 200-day moving averages. Historically, altcoins have only regained strong bullish momentum after Bitcoin has convincingly broken above its all-time high (ATH). Without this confirmation from BTC, capital tends to stay conservative, favoring liquidity and safety over speculation.

In essence, Larsen’s consistent profit-taking and the wider altcoin stagnation highlight the market’s transitional phase. Until Bitcoin reasserts dominance through a clean breakout, most altcoins — including XRP — are likely to face muted inflows and persistent volatility. Investors are now watching whether Bitcoin’s next major move will reignite confidence across the crypto landscape or confirm that the current rally was just another temporary bounce in an uncertain cycle.

XRP Price Analysis: Testing Support as Momentum Fades

XRP continues to trade under pressure, consolidating around the $2.40 zone after failing to reclaim its short-term moving averages. The 3-day chart shows the token struggling below both the 50-day and 100-day moving averages, signaling persistent bearish momentum. The recent rejection near the $2.60–$2.70 area aligns with a key resistance cluster that has consistently capped upside attempts since early October.

Despite the current weakness, XRP has managed to hold above the 200-day moving average, which currently sits near $2.00 — a level that has historically acted as strong dynamic support. If this level fails, the next downside target could lie around $1.80–$1.90, where the previous accumulation zone formed earlier this year.

On the upside, bulls would need to push the price decisively above $2.70 to regain control and confirm a short-term trend reversal. Such a move would likely attract fresh liquidity and shift sentiment toward recovery.

XRP remains in a vulnerable position, with price action suggesting indecision and a lack of strong buying volume. As Bitcoin continues to dictate broader market direction, XRP’s ability to hold above its 200-day moving average will be crucial to avoid deeper losses in the sessions ahead.

Featured image from ChatGPT, chart from TradingView.com

data.title: Crude Oil Skyrockets As U.S. Gets Aggressive With Russia
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Crude oil catapulted on Thursday, extending gains from the past two sessions, as the U.S. takes up the 'sanctions route' against Russia.

WTI Crude Oil for December delivery was last seen trading up by $3.22 (or 5.50%) at $61.72 per barrel.

To curb Russia's efforts to raise revenue for funding its war against Ukraine, the U.S. Treasury Department announced new sanctions targeting Russian oil majors Rosneft and Lukoil along with some of their subsidiaries.

Rosneft and Lukoil are among the biggest corporations listed on the Moscow Stock Exchange, export around 3.1 million barrels of crude oil per day and are worth $50 billion each.

Following this sudden move by the U.S., 27-member states of the European Union adopted the 19th sanctions package against Russia targeting its shadow fleet of oil tankers and banning its imports of Liquefied Natural Gas.

The sanctions include 117 additional vessels from the 'shadow fleet' moving up the total number of blacklisted tankers to around 560.

Russian oil majors, Rosneft and Gazpromneft are on a full transaction ban now.

In the first phase of a Liquefied Natural Gas ban, short-term contracts end after six months, and long-term contracts close from January 1, 2027.

The package also limits the movement of Russian diplomats within the EU.

Earlier this week, U.S. President Donald Trump announced that Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi had offered assurances that India would restrict its purchases of Russian crude. However, India has not officially confirmed Trump's statements so far.

These collective moves are intended to intensify pressure on Russia, as well as drive demand for crude from other sources.

As a result of these concerted moves by the U.S. and the West successively, oil prices jumped as concerns of supply disruption rise.

Meanwhile the Labor Department is scheduled to release its reading on consumer prices on Friday. Money markets are bracing for a reduction in interest rates by the U.S. Federal Reserve at its upcoming October 28-29 meeting due to recent reports suggesting fragility in the U.S. labor market.

U.S.-China trade tension that was set off by China's measures to curb its rare earth mineral exports is waxing and waning.

The leaders of the two nations are set to meet in South Korea by next week. However, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent warned that if China does not change its stance, every critical software exports to China from the U.S. will be inaccessible to China.

The U.S. and China are the world's largest economies and biggest consumers of vital commodities including crude oil. A faceoff between them is pushing oil on the upside.

Crude oil also found support after the U.S. Department of Energy announced plans to buy 1 million barrels of crude oil for delivery to the Strategic Petroleum Reserve.

The DoE intends to take advantage of current low oil prices to help replenish its stockpile. At SPR's 700-million-barrel capacity and its current holdings of 408 million barrels, the intended quantum though symbolic represents the attraction for current low prices.

In the Middle East, as the Trump-backed Gaza Peace Plan is entering its next phase, doubts about Hamas disarming as agreed and who will govern Gaza remain though the situation so far is peaceful.

data.title: Wheat Futures Rise on Hopes for Chinese Buying — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Wheat for December delivery rose 1.8% to $5.13 1/2 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Thursday, supported by a rumor of China's interest in purchasing U.S. wheat.
  • Corn for December delivery rose 1.2% to $4.28 a bushel.
  • Soybeans for November delivery rose 0.9% to $10.44 1/2 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Rumor Has It: Lifting wheat was a rumor that spread around the market that China was looking into purchasing U.S. wheat shipments. While the rumor was enough to move wheat futures, analysts question the validity of the story that China was in the market for one lot of U.S. white wheat. No confirmation of a purchase by China was confirmed Thursday, although the USDA is currently not publishing notices of large-volume flash sales due to the government shutdown.

Game On: News broke in the afternoon that President Trump is scheduled to meet with Chinese leader Xi Jinping next Thursday in a bilateral meeting that will be part of a multi-country trip to Asia. It's a positive sign for grain traders, who anticipate that a trade deal could be nearing with a potential restart of China purchasing U.S. soybean exports. But some remain skeptical that the meeting will yield positive news. "U.S./China tensions appear to be worsening--not improving," said Daniel Flynn of Price Futures Group in a note.

Source of Support: A 5% rise in crude oil prices in response to sanctions placed by the U.S. on major Russian players Rosneft and Lukoil gave support to soybean oil futures on the CBOT, which in turn boosted underlying soybean futures. But soybeans are still feeling the pressure from the ongoing soybean harvest, and the crops being sold to buyers like grain elevators and renewable fuels plants. That pressure is growing weaker as the harvest nears its conclusion, said Doug Bergman of RCM Alternatives in a note.

INSIGHT

Dose of Caution: CBOT grain futures started the day on a tentative note, with traders focused on developments out of China. "Weekend trade negotiations are high stakes with the U.S. threatening to limit software exports to China," said AgResource in a note. For grains, the resumption of U.S. soybean export purchasing by China is the most-important topic.

Building Strength: The dollar has been doing well in the past few days, as markets wait for inflation data tomorrow. BBH analysts, however, expect the Fed to turn more dovish by year end, weakening the greenback. "Progress towards the Fed's 2% inflation goal is stalling," but upside risks to prices aren't materializing, they write. The analysts also note that wage growth has been consistent with 2% inflation, considering non-farm productivity. The WSJ Dollar Index is higher Thursday.

AHEAD

Note: The government shutdown is impacting the timing of USDA reports and data releases.

  • The USDA is scheduled to release its monthly Cold Storage report at 3 p.m. ET Friday.
  • The USDA is scheduled to release its monthly Cattle on Feed report at 3 p.m. ET Friday.
  • The CFTC is scheduled to release its weekly Commitment of Traders report at 3:30 p.m. ET Friday.

Paulo Trevisani contributed to this article.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Oil Futures Rally As U.S. Sanctions Russian Producers — Market Talk
data.description: Crude futures post hefty gains after the Trump administration imposed sanctions on Russia's biggest oil companies, raising expectations that India and China could halt or at least reduce their purchases of Russian oil. "We are back to same questions we have had all year on sanctions: Will they stick? or will the banking/oil trading world figure out a way around them?" Scott Shelton of TP ICAP says in a note. Rosneft and Lukoil have recently been accounting for most of India's roughly 1.6 million b/d of Russian oil imports, he says. If India does stop buying oil from Russia, prices could probably go higher "while an end of the war will probably result in new lows." WTI settles up 5.6% at $61.79 a barrel and Brent rises 5.4% to $65.99.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Wells Fargo Recruits $1 Billion Merrill Lynch Financial Advisor Team — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

Wells Fargo has recruited a $1 billion advisory team in Bellevue, Wash. The Kang, Dime, Tran, Osborne Group joins the Private Client Group at Wells Fargo Advisors, the firm's traditional brokerage channel.

The team joins from Merrill Lynch, where they produced annual revenue of nearly $4.8 million for the most recent year, according to Wells Fargo.

"Their decision to join us reflects our deep capabilities in serving high-net-worth and ultrahigh-net-worth clients, as well as our strong in-market expertise," says Frank Correale, Wells Fargo's Northwest market leader. "We're excited about the value they bring and the impact they will have in the Bellevue area."

Merrill Lynch declined to comment on the departure.

The team is led by Hyeon Kang, who serves as a managing director and private wealth advisor. The practice includes financial advisors Larry Dime, Hung Tran, and Joe Osborne, as well as client associate Kevin Ki.

The recruiting win is one of the latest instances of an advisory team with a large book of business jumping from one large brokerage to another. Wells Fargo last month said it had landed a $1 billion Ameriprise team. That followed the August announcement that it had snagged a $1.7 billion team from Morgan Stanley and the news from February that it had recruited a $1.6 billion practice from UBS, among other moves.

It has also been on the other end, losing advisors to Merrill Lynch, LPL Financial, and others.

Kang is a 23-year veteran of the wealth management industry, according to the online database BrokerCheck. He joined UBS in 2002 as a dually registered investment advisor and broker. From 2007 through 2015, he was registered with Morgan Stanley before jumping to Merrill Lynch. He joined Wells Fargo Oct. 10, completing his tour through the four national brokerage firms known as wirehouses.

Dime, also a dual registrant, broke into the industry in 1995 with Morgan Stanley and moved to Merrill Lynch in 2015. Tran first registered as an advisor and broker with Morgan Stanley in 2012 before joining Merrill Lynch in 2015. Osborne started with Wells Fargo in 2013, but spent less than a year there. After a lapse in registration, he joined Merrill Lynch in 2015 as a dual registrant.

Write to advisor.editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US business borrowing for equipment rises over 8% in September, ELFA says
data.description:
U.S. companies borrowed 8.6% more to finance equipment investments in September compared to a year ago, despite continued political and economic uncertainty, the Equipment Leasing and Finance Association said on Thursday.

New loans, leases and lines of credit signed up by companies in September rose to $10.5 billion on a seasonally adjusted basis, marking a 4% increase from the previous month.

September saw the largest single-month increase in new business volumes in nine months, the report added.

"With the Fed resuming its easing cycle, I expect demand to remain strong and financial conditions to improve further," ELFA President and CEO Leigh Lytle said.

The Washington-based trade association represents financial services companies and manufacturers in the $1.3 trillion U.S. equipment finance sector.

The ELFA CapEx Finance Index of leasing and finance activity is based on a 25-member survey, including Bank of America as well as the financing units of Caterpillar , Dell Technologies , Siemens AG , Canon and Volvo AB .

The Equipment Leasing & Finance Foundation, ELFA's non-profit affiliate, sees its October confidence index relatively unchanged at 60.1 from 59.9 in September. A reading above 50 indicates a positive business outlook.

data.title: Suncor reports restart of units at Montreal refinery
data.description:
Suncor Energy on Thursday reported the restart of units in sequence at its 137,000 barrels-per-day Montreal refinery.

On Monday, a power outage had prompted a shutdown at the refinery.

"This restart should last a few days. During this period, the flame from the flare may be more visible," the company said in a notice to the community.

data.title: Citi’s CEO bonus is another game of catch-up
data.description:
By Stephen Gandel

The bank has lagged peers in results and its boss’s pay, with Jane Fraser earning $23 mln less than rivals since 2021. Like them, she’s adding board chair to her role, with extra compensation. If she keeps narrowing the performance gap, it makes sense – for Wall Street, at least.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn and X.

CONTEXT NEWS

Citigroup disclosed on October 22 that its board had voted to name the bank’s CEO Jane Fraser as executive chair. It will be the first time the lender will combine both roles since the 2008 financial crisis.

In addition, Citi awarded Fraser a one-time bonus valued at $25 million, as well as an additional roughly 1 million stock options, that will pay out and vest, respectively, starting in 2028.

data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Futures Settles Lower on Storage Data — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. natural gas extends its retreat as last week's storage build came in above expectations. "It appears that upside momentum might have run out over the past 24 hours, and today's bearish storage data is helping to keep downward pressure on pricing," Andy Huenefeld of Pinebrook Energy Advisors says in a note. Natural gas in underground storage rose by 87 Bcf last week to 3,808 Bcf, extending the surplus over the five-year average to 164 Bcf from 154 Bcf the week before. An 81 Bcf injection was expected, according to a WSJ survey of analysts. "Only two weeks remain in the traditional injection season, but it would not be out of the question for storage to continue to grow through mid-November if weather cooperates," Huenefeld adds. Nymex natural gas settles down 3.1% at $3.344/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Judge says Musk cannot use advice-of-counsel defense in Twitter lawsuit
data.description:
By David Thomas

Elon Musk cannot cite past legal advice he received from two prominent law firms as part of his defense in a lawsuit stemming from his delayed disclosure of his stake in Twitter in 2022, a Manhattan federal magistrate judge ruled on Thursday.

U.S. Magistrate Judge Gabriel Gorenstein said Musk and his co-defendants are "precluded from offering evidence or argument that they relied on the advice of counsel or had a good faith belief in the lawfulness of their conduct after they consulted counsel on April 1, 2022."

The ruling concerns advice Musk received from Quinn Emanuel, which is also defending him in the lawsuit, and from McDermott Will & Emery, which he hired days before he amended a key filing in April 2022 that revealed his 9.2% stake in Twitter.

Musk did not immediately respond to a request for comment, nor did spokespersons for Quinn Emanuel and McDermott.

A lawyer for the plaintiff, an Oklahoma firefighters pension fund, declined to comment.

The lawsuit accuses Musk and others of defrauding Twitter shareholders who sold stock in the social media company at artificially low prices because they were kept in the dark about Musk's growing share.

Musk eventually purchased Twitter for $44 billion in October 2022, and renamed it X.

Musk has said he had intended to reveal his 9.2% stake in Twitter at the end of 2022, but disclosed it promptly after realizing he misunderstood the U.S. Securities & Exchange Commission's disclosure rule. He has denied wrongdoing and said this was "not a scheme to defraud."

The pension fund had pressed Musk and his co-defendants to make explicit whether they planned to rely on legal counsel’s involvement to argue they acted in good faith.

The fund in August warned against Musk using a "classic 'sword and shield' strategy" by invoking their lawyers' advice as a defense while also refusing to turn over documents relating to that defense, citing attorney-client privilege.

Alex Spiro, a top lawyer at Quinn Emanuel representing Musk, said in an August 20 letter that they "repeatedly have told plaintiff — both orally and in writing — that defendants are not asserting an advice-of-counsel defense in this action."

data.title: UBS' Aileen Schruth Explains How She Built an Advisory Practice by Helping Clients Navigate Divorce — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Weld Royal

UBS private wealth advisor Aileen Schruth and her team have built a $5.5 billion practice by mastering one of the most emotionally charged — and financially consequential — niches in wealth management: financial planning for clients going through a divorce. Schruth's journey illustrates how deep expertise can change client relationships, empower individuals in turmoil, and drive growth for an advisory business.

In our latest episode of The Way Forward: Next Generation podcast , Schruth, 43, talks about why she decided to specialize in divorce financial planning, how she followed a well-defined route — pursuing certifications and aligning herself with a team experienced in helping clients navigate divorce — and why she likes her "Iron Lady" nickname. "I play the bad guy all the time. I work with many different generations of clients. One will say their son or daughter is getting divorced. I'll say, 'great, who's talking about the prenup?'"

Schruth's path to wealth management started in 2003 in municipal bond trading. She liked the pace of Wall Street, but quickly realized she didn't want to trade munis. Schruth heard a wealth manager speak at a networking event and discovered her calling.

Building a career through a niche. Schruth soon landed an entry-level wealth management role and was handed a phone book. "I dialed and dialed and dialed," she recalls. "I would have to make hundreds of calls to get someone on the phone." She invited prospects to lunch and some would just come for the food, a few would take a meeting and fewer still would become clients.

"I realized that the phone call process was not what I wanted," she says. "I needed an expertise." She decided to focus on certifications. She became a Chartered Retirement Planning Counselor, then earned her CFP, and next obtained a Certified Divorce Financial Analyst designation.

She also moved to UBS and an established, female-led team that worked with a lot of women, some of whom were going through divorce. They attracted others who had just split up with their spouses and wanted a new wealth manager.

Structures to limit stress. Through study and client work, Schruth refined her specialization. She built deep relationships, learning about clients' hopes, fears — and business operations. "You need to protect your balance sheet, and that can make things easier down the road," she says.

Schruth believes disciplined thinking, and structured legal documents are crucial given the passionate feelings and financial complexities that surround divorce. Asset division stirs emotion.

"There could be items on the balance sheet — the primary home, the beach house, art, or company stock that you accumulated — that you don't want your spouse to get," Schruth says.

Her so-called "Three L's" framework — liquidity, longevity, and legacy — provides a system for decision-making around short-term cash and expenses, sustaining a lifetime income, and ensuring wealth transfers according to wishes. This system also helps to combat fear-driven inertia, which is all too common. "The liquidity bucket can't be too big, " she cautions. "If fear drives you to preserve every dollar in cash, you miss out on growth and outpacing inflation."

Inheritors vs. creators. What distinguishes Schruth's book of business is her dual client base. About half are next-generation inheritors focused on preservation, and most others are wealth creators, such as entrepreneurs and executives, who are fixated on financial freedom and strategic planning to exit or evolve their careers.

One of Schruth's clients calls her the "Iron Lady." Another uses a term unprintable in Barron's Advisor. She's proud of the monikers. She believes her rigorous yet collaborative method shifts clients from passive recipients of advice into active partners in executing their financial plans. "If you tell me you want to retire by 50, I'll tell you how to get there, but you've got to do the work," Schruth says. "And I check in."

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Soybean, corn futures hit 1-month highs as crude oil soars
data.description:
By Julie Ingwersen

U.S. soybean and corn futures rose on Thursday to one-month highs, spurred by a jump in crude oil <CLc1> following U.S. sanctions on Russia's two biggest oil firms along with hopes for progress in U.S.-Chinese trade talks.

Wheat futures climbed more than 1%, supported by firming domestic cash markets and possible fund short-covering, analysts said.

As of 12:50 p.m. CDT (1750 GMT), Chicago Board of Trade November soybeans (SX25) were up 8 cents at $10.42-3/4 per bushel after reaching $10.43-1/2, the contract's highest since September 19.

CBOT December corn (CZ25) was up 5 cents at $4.28 a bushel while December wheat (WZ25) was up 8-1/2 cents at $5.12-1/4 a bushel.

Crude oil surged 5% after the U.S. imposed sanctions on major Russian suppliers Rosneft <ROSN.MM> and Lukoil <LKOH.MM> over the Ukraine war. Soybeans and corn sometimes follow oil prices as the crops are widely processed for use in biofuel.

CBOT December soyoil futures (BOZ25) were up 0.83 cent, or 1.66%, at 50.90 cents per pound.

The soybean market also found support from prospects for a U.S.-China trade deal after their standoff this year halted massive U.S. soybean sales to China.

U.S. President Donald Trump maintained an upbeat tone about reaching agreements with his Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping when they are due to meet in South Korea next week, reiterating that the resumption of soybean purchases is a key area of discussion.

Some analysts noted brisk demand for soy from buyers other than China as well as domestic soy processors.

"The cash markets are really strong, not only in the interior (Midwest) but also for export. ... We are the cheapest origin versus Brazil," said Tom Fritz, a partner with EFG Group in Chicago.

Similarly, corn futures found support from cash-market strength. Cash basis bids for corn have firmed at a few locations this week, reflecting a lack of farmer selling and uncertainty about the size of the crop, given anecdotal reports from farmers of underwhelming yields, Fritz said.

"We've got a fairly decent (corn) export market and a great domestic market. And the chatter continues about 'how small is the yield,'" Fritz said.

The U.S. Department of Agriculture has not released updated U.S. production estimates since mid-September due to the ongoing government shutdown.

data.title: MSC INDUSTRIAL DIRECT CO INC SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
MSC Industrial Direct Co., Inc., a leading North American distributor of metalworking and maintenance, repair, and operations (MRO) products and services, has released its annual Form 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

MSC Industrial Direct Co., Inc. reported the following key financial metrics for the fiscal year:

  • Net Sales: $3,769.5 million, a decrease of 1.3% from the prior fiscal year, primarily due to lower sales volume and unfavorable foreign exchange impacts, partially offset by improved pricing and net sales from recent acquisitions.
  • Gross Profit: $1,536.1 million, a decrease of 2.3% from the prior fiscal year, with a gross profit margin of 40.8% compared to 41.2% in the previous year, mainly due to lower sales volume and higher inventory costs.
  • Income from Operations: $301.6 million, a decrease of 22.8% from the prior fiscal year, with operating expenses increasing as a percentage of net sales due to higher payroll costs and investments in digital initiatives.
  • Net Income: $197.8 million, a decrease of 22.7% from the prior fiscal year, impacted by lower sales volume and increased operating expenses.
  • Net Income Per Common Share - Basic: $3.57, down from $4.60 in the prior fiscal year.
  • Net Income Per Common Share - Diluted: $3.57, down from $4.58 in the prior fiscal year.

Business Highlights

MSC Industrial Direct Co., Inc. demonstrated robust business performance across various segments and geographies:

  • Revenue Segments: The company serves a diverse range of customers, including individual machine shops, Fortune 1000 companies, and government agencies. It offers approximately 2.5 million active SKUs through various channels, including E-commerce, catalogs, and customer care centers.
  • Geographical Performance: MSC operates in the United States, Canada, Mexico, and the United Kingdom, with a sophisticated network of fulfillment centers and warehouses. The U.S. market remains a significant contributor to revenue, with next-day delivery available nationwide for qualifying orders.
  • Sales Units: As of August 30, 2025, MSC had 29,611 vending machines in service, an increase from 27,003 the previous year. The company also expanded its in-plant programs to 411 locations, up from 342.
  • New Product Launches: MSC continues to expand its product offerings, including the acquisition of intellectual property assets from Schmitz Manufacturing Research & Technology LLC, enhancing its technological capabilities.
  • New Production Launches: The company has invested in improving its distribution efficiency, utilizing robotic packing solutions and order-picking systems in its fulfillment centers to enhance productivity and safety.
  • Future Outlook: MSC's strategy focuses on maintaining growth momentum, increasing focus on core customers and OEM fasteners, and driving productivity improvements. The company plans to leverage advanced analytics to enhance supply chain performance and digital capabilities.

Strategic Initiatives

MSC Industrial Direct Co., Inc. has undertaken several strategic initiatives to drive long-term growth and profitability:

  • Mission Critical Initiative: The company continued its 'Mission Critical' initiative, focusing on market share capture and improved profitability. This included enhancing digital and E-commerce capabilities, diversifying the customer base, and leveraging advanced analytics to improve supply chain performance.
  • Capital Management: MSC generated $333.7 million of cash from operations and reduced total debt by $23.1 million, primarily through net payments on credit facilities and private placement debt. It repurchased $39.3 million of Class A Common Stock and paid $189.7 million in regular cash dividends. The company also disposed of the Columbus CFC for $32.0 million.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to maintain momentum from its 'Mission Critical' program, focusing on core customers and OEM fasteners, and driving productivity improvements. It intends to leverage investments in advanced analytics and digital upgrades to unlock productivity. The company anticipates increased pressure from tariffs in fiscal year 2026 and will continue to monitor economic conditions for impacts on its business.

Challenges and Risks

MSC Industrial Direct Co., Inc. faces several challenges and risks in its operating environment:

  • Competitive Landscape: MSC operates in a highly competitive and fragmented industry, facing competition from traditional and online distributors.
  • Supply Chain Disruptions: The company is exposed to risks related to supply chain disruptions, which could impact its ability to deliver products timely.
  • Technological Risks: MSC's reliance on technological innovation and digital platforms presents risks if these systems fail to meet customer expectations or if competitors offer superior solutions.
  • Economic Conditions: MSC's business is heavily influenced by the economic conditions affecting its customers, particularly in cyclical markets. Economic downturns can lead to reduced demand for MSC's products and services.
  • Commodity and Labor Prices: The company faces risks from volatile commodity, energy, and labor prices, which can impact operating margins.
  • Regulatory Compliance: Regulatory compliance, especially as a supplier to the public sector, imposes additional costs and potential liabilities.
  • Credit Risk: The company's exposure to credit risk from customers, inventory management challenges, and potential supply chain disruptions are significant operational risks.
  • Market Risks: MSC is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in interest rates and foreign currency exchange rates, which could impact its financial results. The company is also subject to risks from changes in trade policies and tariffs, which could increase product costs and affect market access.

SEC Filing: MSC INDUSTRIAL DIRECT CO INC [ MSM ] - 10-K - Oct. 23, 2025

data.title: Super Micro Cuts 1Q Outlook, Sending Shares Tumbling — Update
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Super Micro Computer cut its revenue outlook for its fiscal first quarter after some customers delayed taking delivery of their orders.

Shares fell 8.5% Thursday to $48.05. The stock is down more than 8% in the past three months, but up 58% year-to-date.

The maker of servers used for generative artificial intelligence now expects revenue of about $5 billion for the three months ended Sept. 30, down from its prior outlook of $6 billion to $7 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet had forecast $6.49 billion.

Chief Executive Charles Liang said the company expects to make up the shortfall later in the year as the delayed orders shift into the second quarter.

"We see customer demand accelerating, and we are gaining AI share," Liang said, highlighting strong interest in Super Micro's newly released artificial-intelligence liquid-cooled systems.

The company reaffirmed its full-year revenue target of at least $33 billion, above Wall Street's estimate of $31.94 billion.

Super Micro has been mired in problems. The beleaguered company in August tempered its fiscal-year outlook, citing order delays.

Earlier this year, Super Micro filed two quarterly reports from 2024, as well as its annual report for that year, with the Securities and Exchange Commission, bringing it back into compliance with financial reporting requirements and removing an issue that had hung over the company for months.

The filings came after Ernst & Young last year resigned as Super Micro's auditor, as the Big Four accounting firm cited that it could no longer rely on the company's representations and didn't want to be associated with financial statements prepared by its leadership.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com
